title: Getting started
summary: Python is a readable language often used for scripting, data work, and web backends. You run code line by line or save it in a .py file.
bullets: Indentation (spaces) defines blocks instead of curly braces.
The interactive REPL lets you try one line at a time; scripts run many lines from a file.
Comments start with # and are ignored by Python.

title: Variables and types
summary: Names point to values. Common built-in types include integers, floats, strings, and booleans.
bullets: Use = to bind a name to a value: age = 21
int holds whole numbers; float holds decimals.
str holds text in quotes; bool is True or False.
type(x) shows what kind of value x is.
examples:
name = "Ada"
score = 98.5
active = True
print(name, score, active)

title: Operators
summary: Arithmetic and comparison operators behave like math class, with a few Python-specific extras.
bullets: + - * / perform basic math; // is integer division.
% gives the remainder (modulo).
== != < > <= >= compare values and return True or False.
and, or, not combine boolean conditions.

title: Control flow
summary: if/elif/else pick branches; for and while repeat work until a condition changes.
bullets: if condition: runs a block when condition is True.
elif adds more tests; else runs when nothing matched.
for item in iterable: visits each item.
while condition: keeps looping while condition stays True.
break exits a loop early; continue skips to the next iteration.

title: Data structures
summary: Lists, dicts, tuples, and sets organize multiple values in different ways.
bullets: list: ordered, mutable sequence. Example: items = [1, 2, 3]
dict: key to value mapping. Example: user = {"name": "Ada"}
tuple: ordered, immutable sequence. Example: point = (3, 4)
set: unordered unique values. Example: tags = {"py", "dev"}
Index lists and strings with [0]; dict values with ["key"].

title: Functions
summary: def groups reusable steps. Parameters receive arguments; return sends a value back.
bullets: def name(params): introduces a function.
Call it with name(arg1, arg2).
return exits and optionally provides a result.
Local variables inside a function do not leak out unless returned.
examples:
def greet(name):
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

message = greet("world")
print(message)

title: Useful patterns
summary: These idioms appear constantly in real Python code and in PyTyping exercises.
bullets: List comprehensions build lists in one line: [x * 2 for x in nums]
enumerate(iterable) gives (index, item) pairs in loops.
with open(path) as f: safely opens files and closes them automatically.
class Name: defines a type with data (attributes) and behavior (methods).

title: Next steps
summary: When you are ready, switch to the Exercises tab and type real snippets. Use this guide anytime you forget a concept.
bullets: Start with beginner exercises if Python is new.
Read each exercise breakdown after typing to connect syntax to meaning.
Official docs go deeper on every topic listed here.
